primary_hypothesis: A Shift-B-specific factor acting within the assay pathway shared by samples and sealed controls, such as the untested shared reagent lot, caused the elevated abnormal results.
rival_hypothesis: Residual contamination on cleaned sample-contact surfaces caused the Shift-B sample abnormalities.
predicted_discrimination: A substantial Shift-B increase among sealed controls, especially one comparable to the sample shift gap, would support the shared-assay-factor candidate and disfavor the sample-surface-contamination rival because sealed controls do not contact those surfaces. No sealed-control gap would disfavor the candidate and leave the surface-contamination rival viable.
bounded_conclusion: Within the supplied 100-result records for each shift, the same elevation from 1 abnormal result in Shift A to 10 in Shift B appears in both samples and sealed controls. Because the sealed controls do not contact the cleaned sample surfaces, the records support a Shift-B factor shared by the assay pathways and reject residual sample-surface contamination as a sufficient explanation for this observed pattern; they do not identify which shared factor caused it.
residual_uncertainty: The shared reagent-lot explanation remains untested, and the records do not distinguish it from another Shift-B-wide factor such as instrument, environment, handling, or result-classification effects.
recommended_consequence: Quarantine the affected Shift-B results and associated materials for cause-focused review; compare reagent lots and other shared Shift-B assay inputs before release or corrective action, and do not assign discipline because no disciplinary authority or person-specific causal evidence is supplied.
